sub_question.question: What empirical scaling laws govern transformer training loss?

web_search.content: Empirical work shows transformer training loss follows a power law in model size, dataset size, and compute (Kaplan et al. 2020).

source.title: Scaling Laws Overview
source.snippet: Training loss follows a power law in model size, dataset size, and compute.

arxiv_search.content: Hoffmann et al. (2022) revised compute-optimal scaling, recommending more training tokens per parameter than Kaplan et al.

source.title: Chinchilla compute-optimal scaling
source.snippet: Compute-optimal LLMs need more tokens per parameter than previously believed.

sub_question.question: How do approximate-nearest-neighbor index types compare for vector databases?

web_search.content: HNSW offers low-latency recall trade-offs; IVF-PQ trades recall for memory; DiskANN scales to billions on SSDs.

source.title: ANN index comparison
source.snippet: HNSW, IVF-PQ, and DiskANN differ in recall, memory, and latency trade-offs.

sub_question.question: Why does retrieval-augmented generation reduce hallucinations?

web_search.content: RAG grounds generated text in retrieved passages, reducing unsupported claims.

source.title: RAG primer
source.snippet: RAG grounds generation in retrieved passages.

arxiv_search.content: Lewis et al. (2020) show that grounding generations in retrieved passages reduces hallucination rates.

source.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
source.snippet: Grounding generation in retrieval reduces hallucinations.
